transcript:
Right now you're looking at a tidy grid of hard-edged shapes — circles, squares, rectangles in five confident colors, sitting in loose columns like something carefully laid out. Watch it for ninety seconds and that confidence erodes into a glowing haze.

Here's the part I love: in the code, those original shapes are literally classed as "authored" — box-shadowed, opaque, anchored in place. But every one of them spawns children through a function called "spawn descendant," and each generation's hue, saturation, and size gets nudged by randomness — the comment calls it losing "authorial specificity." By the third generation, the class name is just "dissolved." The artist even wrote a line saying the mark "remembers what it came from, even as it drifts" — so the lineage exists in memory, but nothing on screen lets you trace it back.

What gets me is that the original shapes never move. They're described as anchors — fixed points that quietly become irrelevant as everything they spawned drifts, blurs, and buries them in light. The authorship isn't erased. It's just outnumbered.
